refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with latency that permits safe remote operation by a tele-operator.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation capability, and (2) ensuring latency is compatible with safety. The refined version separates the functional intent (enable teleoperation communication) from the performance constraint (latency compatible with safe operation). The acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ 150 ms) is a measurable design target that flows from the safety requirement, not part of the FR itself. The FR now resides purely in the problem domain—what capability must exist—without prescribing how latency will be achieved (network architecture, compression, edge computing, etc.).
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency (sensor input to actuator command output) under agreed network conditions. Validate that measured p95 latency ≤ 150 ms. Conduct safety case review with Safety Manager and Tele-operator to confirm latency supports safe operation per worksite operational policies.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The NGHE solution shall transmit tele-operator control inputs to remote equipment actuators.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The NGHE solution shall transmit equipment sensor data and state feedback to the tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The NGHE solution shall maintain end-to-end communication latency within bounds that support safe remote operation as defined by the worksite safety plan.
refined_fr.notes: The original FR is not atomic; it bundles capability (teleoperation support) with a performance constraint (latency compatibility). The three sub-FRs are atomic: each addresses a single functional obligation. FR-008.3 explicitly links the latency performance to the safety domain, making the safety dependency visible. The acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ 150 ms) should be captured as a Design Parameter or Performance Specification derived from safety analysis, not embedded in the FR.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement a deterministic, low-latency communication pipeline with end-to-end latency budgeting across acquisition, transmission, processing, and actuation stages, with jitter control and priority queuing to meet p95 ≤ 150 ms.
DP2: Establish a dual-path communication architecture (primary + failover) with automatic switchover logic and latency monitoring to ensure safe operation under variable network conditions.
DP3: Decompose teleoperation into independent functional channels (command, feedback, safety) with separate latency budgets and isolation mechanisms to prevent cross-channel interference.
DP4: Employ adaptive quality-of-service (QoS) negotiation with graceful degradation of non-critical telemetry while preserving command and safety feedback within latency bounds.

DP5: Implement edge-local autonomy with operator-in-the-loop validation, where time-critical decisions are made locally and operator commands are validated asynchronously to decouple operator latency from safety-critical actions.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Decompose teleoperation into independent functional channels (command, feedback, safety) with separate latency budgets and isolation mechanisms to prevent cross-channel interference.

potential_sources: Command and feedback channels may share underlying transport infrastructure, creating implicit coupling if not explicitly isolated at the DP level.
Safety channel isolation must be enforced at the IM layer (separate hardware paths, prioritized queuing, or dedicated network segments) to prevent latency coupling.
Operator feedback loop (visual/haptic) and command execution may exhibit feedback coupling if latency budgets are not independently managed.
coupling_check.notes: DP3 achieves low coupling by logically separating concerns (command intent, state feedback, safety assertions) and allowing independent latency budgets. Coupling risk is deferred to IM layer decisions (e.g., shared vs. dedicated transport). This supports modularity and testability.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Network conditions are characterized and stable enough to support deterministic latency budgeting; variability is bounded and measurable.
ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan defines acceptable latency thresholds for each functional channel (command, feedback, safety) and operator training ensures safe behavior within those bounds.
RISK: If network conditions are highly variable or unpredictable, latency budgets may be violated, requiring fallback to DP5 (edge autonomy) or DP4 (graceful degradation).
RISK: Operator training and situational awareness are critical; DP3 alone does not guarantee safe operation if the operator misinterprets delayed feedback or issues unsafe commands.
RISK: Synchronization between independent channels may introduce subtle timing bugs if not carefully managed at the IM layer (e.g., command executed before corresponding safety check is received).
RISK: Latency p95 ≤ 150 ms is a tight constraint; any single-point failure in the communication pipeline (e.g., congested router, overloaded edge node) could violate it.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) the network conditions under which p95 ≤ 150 ms must be met (e.g., LTE with X Mbps, fiber with Y ms RTT, or hybrid); (2) the definition of 'safe remote operation' in terms of operator feedback requirements (e.g., video latency, haptic feedback, state confirmation); (3) the role of edge-local autonomy or failover logic in maintaining safety if latency is exceeded; (4) the acceptable degradation modes (e.g., reduced telemetry, command buffering, operator override). Additionally, map each functional channel (command, feedback, safety) to explicit latency budgets and acceptance criteria to enable independent DP evaluation and IM design.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates the decomposition of teleoperation into three independent functional channels (command, feedback, safety) with isolated latency budgets, cross-channel interference prevention mechanisms, and channel-specific quality-of-service policies.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and mission directives; routed through command channel with dedicated latency budget.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state, sensor feedback, and operational status; routed through feedback channel with independent latency budget.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional safety-critical signaling; safety channel operates with highest priority and strictest isolation to prevent interference from command/feedback channels.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Physical/logical network transport; Teleoperation module manages channel multiplexing, prioritization, and isolation at the link layer.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. The DP directly describes the internal architecture of teleoperation (channel decomposition, latency budgeting, isolation). All three channels (command, feedback, safety) are logically contained within this module's responsibility. Minimal diffusion: only network transport and safety alert signaling cross the boundary, both via well-defined interfaces.

Poor fit. Safety Services would naturally own the safety channel logic, but the DP encompasses command and feedback channels as well. Placing the entire decomposition here violates separation of concerns and creates tight coupling between safety-specific logic and general teleoperation orchestration. Command and feedback channels would diffuse into Vehicle or other modules.

Very poor fit. Vehicle is the subject of teleoperation, not the orchestrator. The DP describes teleoperation control logic, not vehicle dynamics or actuation. Placing channel decomposition here inverts the dependency and creates high diffusion of teleoperation concerns across vehicle subsystems.

Moderate fit but overly broad. NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. While it could technically host the Teleoperation module, assigning the DP to NGHE Solution directly bypasses the natural architectural hierarchy and obscures ownership. Better to assign to the more specific Teleoperation module and let NGHE Solution orchestrate it.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural and most specific boundary for DP3. It owns the decomposition logic, channel isolation mechanisms, latency budget allocation, and cross-channel interference prevention. The three channels (command, feedback, safety) are internal design decisions within this module. External dependencies (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimal, indicating low coupling and high cohesion.
containment_risks: Risk: If latency budgeting or isolation mechanisms require real-time kernel support or network QoS policies, those concerns may diffuse into Network Link or Vehicle firmware. Mitigation: Define clear latency and isolation contracts at the Teleoperation boundary; delegate enforcement to lower-level modules via interface specifications.

Risk: Safety channel logic may overlap with Safety Services module responsibilities. Mitigation: Establish clear ownership: Teleoperation owns channel routing and isolation; Safety Services owns safety decision logic and alert generation. Use SafetyAlert interface to decouple.

Risk: Command channel may require Vehicle-specific protocol handling (e.g., actuator command encoding). Mitigation: Teleoperation owns the abstract command channel; Vehicle owns protocol translation via Tasking interface contract.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 directly addresses the FR by decomposing teleoperation into independent functional channels with separate latency budgets and isolation mechanisms. This design approach is sound for enabling safe remote operation: command channel handles operator directives, feedback channel provides situational awareness, and safety channel enforces critical constraints. The separation of concerns aligns with the latency requirement (p95 ≤ 150 ms) by allowing per-channel optimization and preventing cross-channel interference that could degrade safety-critical signaling.

diffusion_risks: Latency budget enforcement may diffuse into Network Link module if QoS policies or real-time scheduling are required. Mitigation: Define explicit latency contracts at Teleoperation boundary; Network Link implements enforcement via interface specification.

Safety channel isolation logic could overlap with Safety Services if safety decision-making is not cleanly separated from channel routing. Mitigation: Teleoperation owns channel isolation and routing; Safety Services owns alert generation and safety logic. SafetyAlert interface enforces this boundary.

Command protocol translation (e.g., operator command → vehicle actuator encoding) may diffuse into Vehicle module if not abstracted. Mitigation: Teleoperation owns abstract command semantics; Vehicle owns protocol-specific translation via Tasking interface contract.

Network multiplexing and prioritization logic could diffuse across multiple modules if not centralized. Current design contains this well within Teleoperation, but implementation must ensure no duplication in Network Link or Vehicle firmware.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 95% containment score with minimal diffusion (5%). The three channels (command, feedback, safety) are logically cohesive and owned by this module. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimal, indicating strong encapsulation. The module boundary is appropriate for the DP scope. Risk mitigation strategies are clear and actionable.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion (p95 latency ≤ 150 ms) is measurable and can be verified through end-to-end testing under agreed network conditions. Channel-specific latency budgets can be allocated and monitored independently. The Teleoperation module's interface contracts (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) provide clear measurement points for latency validation. Isolation mechanisms can be verified through fault injection and cross-channel interference testing.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR requirement maps to DP3 (channel decomposition with latency budgets and isolation) and is allocated to the Teleoperation IM boundary. Ownership is unambiguous: Teleoperation owns the decomposition logic and channel management. Dependencies on Safety Services (safety decision logic), Vehicle (command execution), and Network Link (transport) are explicit and decoupled via interfaces. The worksite safety plan and operational policies should be enforced through the SafetyAlert channel and Safety Services integration, with clear responsibility boundaries.

recommended_revisions: Formalize latency budget allocation across the three channels (command, feedback, safety) with explicit time margins for each. Document the allocation rationale and how it satisfies the p95 ≤ 150 ms constraint.

Define the SafetyAlert interface contract explicitly: specify message types, priority levels, response time requirements, and failure modes. Clarify the boundary between Teleoperation's channel isolation and Safety Services' alert generation.

Specify the isolation mechanisms (e.g., separate queues, priority scheduling, bandwidth reservation) at the Teleoperation module level. Document how these prevent cross-channel interference under network congestion or failure scenarios.

Create a detailed interface specification for the Network Link boundary, including QoS requirements, multiplexing strategy, and prioritization rules. Ensure Teleoperation's channel logic does not duplicate network-layer concerns.

Document the command protocol abstraction at the Tasking interface to prevent diffusion of vehicle-specific encoding logic into Teleoperation. Define the contract clearly so Vehicle module owns protocol translation.

Add acceptance criteria for isolation verification: e.g., 'Safety channel latency remains ≤ X ms even when command/feedback channels are saturated' and 'Cross-channel interference is < Y% under worst-case network conditions.'

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The Teleoperation module achieves 95% containment with minimal diffusion risk, and the acceptance criterion (p95 latency ≤ 150 ms) is measurable and verifiable. However, the recommended revisions reveal that critical sub-level design decisions remain unresolved: (1) latency budget allocation across three channels lacks formal specification, (2) SafetyAlert interface contract is underspecified, (3) isolation mechanisms are conceptual rather than concrete, and (4) command protocol abstraction boundaries need clarification. Given high safety criticality, these must be decomposed and validated before implementation. Proceeding to next-level sub-FRs will expose and resolve these dependencies while maintaining traceability to the parent FR.
next_level_sub_frs: FR3.1: Allocate and enforce per-channel latency budgets (command, feedback, safety) such that p95 latency for each channel ≤ allocated budget and aggregate p95 ≤ 150 ms.
FR3.2: Isolate command, feedback, and safety channels to prevent cross-channel interference (latency, bandwidth, priority) under network congestion and failure scenarios.
FR3.3: Translate operator commands to abstract vehicle-agnostic semantics and validate command safety constraints before transmission to Vehicle module.
FR3.4: Aggregate vehicle telemetry and safety alerts into unified feedback stream with priority-based ordering and loss-resilient delivery to operator.
FR3.5: Enforce safety-critical constraints (e.g., geofence, speed limits, emergency stop) via dedicated safety channel with guaranteed latency and isolation from command/feedback channels.
FR3.6: Multiplex and prioritize three logical channels over shared network link with QoS enforcement and fallback behavior under congestion.

revise.why: Recommended revisions require explicit DP refinement at the next level: (1) DP3.1 must specify latency budget allocation strategy (e.g., command 50 ms, feedback 60 ms, safety 40 ms with margins), (2) DP3.2 must define isolation mechanisms (separate queues, priority scheduling, bandwidth reservation), (3) DP3.3 must formalize command protocol abstraction and safety validation logic, (4) DP3.4 must specify telemetry aggregation and priority ordering rules, (5) DP3.5 must detail safety constraint enforcement and channel guarantees, and (6) DP3.6 must define multiplexing strategy and QoS policies. These DPs are logically sound but require concrete specification to guide implementation and verification.

assumptions: Network link is shared by all three channels; QoS policies can enforce per-channel prioritization.
Safety Services module is responsible for safety decision logic (alert generation); Teleoperation owns channel routing and isolation.
Vehicle module owns protocol-specific command encoding; Teleoperation owns abstract command semantics.
Operator feedback loop tolerance is ≤ 150 ms p95; safety channel tolerance is stricter (to be defined in FR3.5).
Network conditions are characterized by agreed SLA (e.g., bandwidth, jitter, packet loss); latency budgets assume these conditions.
Isolation mechanisms (e.g., separate queues) are implementable within Teleoperation module without requiring changes to Network Link or Vehicle interfaces.
open_risks: Latency budget allocation may be infeasible if network SLA does not support per-channel QoS; mitigation required at FR3.6 level.
Safety channel isolation under extreme congestion (e.g., 90%+ network utilization) may require hardware-level guarantees (e.g., dedicated network slice); feasibility must be validated.
Command protocol abstraction may leak vehicle-specific concerns into Teleoperation if Vehicle module does not cleanly own encoding logic; interface contract at FR3.3 must be explicit.
Cross-channel interference testing (acceptance criterion in recommended revisions) requires fault injection infrastructure; test harness design must be planned early.
Operator command validation (FR3.3) may introduce latency; budget impact must be quantified and included in FR3.1 allocation.
evidence_needed: Formal latency budget allocation table with per-channel targets, margins, and justification (FR3.1).
Isolation mechanism specification (queue depths, priority levels, bandwidth reservation) with worst-case latency analysis (FR3.2).
Command protocol abstraction specification and interface contract with Vehicle module (FR3.3).
Telemetry aggregation and priority ordering rules with loss-resilience strategy (FR3.4).
Safety constraint enforcement specification and safety channel latency guarantee (FR3.5).
Network multiplexing and QoS policy specification with fallback behavior under congestion (FR3.6).
End-to-end latency measurement plan and acceptance test cases for each sub-FR.
Fault injection test plan for cross-channel interference and isolation verification.
Interface contracts (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) formalized with message types, timing, and failure modes.
